Trans women who pass are an uncountable category unless you ask, because passing makes them visually inseparable from cis women.

Actually I said it was based on their obviously masculine features, not them announcing their trans identity. And I also said I doubt there are so many 100% cis-female-looking transwomen out there, I'm getting fooled left and right everyday.

> Yes, it is difficult for many, maybe even most, trans women to pass, especially if they transitioned after puberty.

That's basically my point. The rest is just semantics.

> But to put an actual number on it based on observation is naive, because your observations are inherently incomplete.

I said it was a ballpark estimate, and I already conceded that to another commenters. Obviously, I can't know 100% of them. It is a good faith guess, however.

Well of course you're only *noticing* the ones that you can spot, and so you assume the proportion that you can't spot is very low.  But how do you know how many transwomen you interacted with without knowing they were trans?

Basically, your number *could* be right, but it's very unlikely because (by definition) you're ignoring the cases where you never found out you were talking to a trans person.

message:
I lived in Moz for four months when I was just out of college. After about 3 months, i was more than ready to get back to the U.S. Your quality of life will drastically drop. You'll quickly notice all the things you take for granted living in a 1st world country (eg. The constant threat of malaria, which you will contract if there for a long period. Also, police corruption. Driving down the road at the speed limit? Doesn't matter, police pull you over with an old speed on their radar gun and threaten to take you to jail if you don't pay them. Happened on every drive between South Africa and Moz). 

Oh yeah, and as to corruption, how about having the local drug dealer and a few of his friends pick a fight with you and beat you the F up since you're outnumbered (careful to not get blood on you, AIDS and all). Next day you'll get a visit from the police looking for a bribe in exchange for no jail time, since you're the foreigner with money. Oh ya, and the cop kicks back to the drug dealer who beat you up, who will come to your bar the next day to laugh at you.

What happens when you have car trouble? Good luck getting a spare part. And when you do it'll be a shitty Chinese knockoff part that won't last long, and are you gonna be your own mechanic, or trust some rando with your truck?


Honestly, I hate to characterize someone off some text, but you come off as a sheltered, spoiled, rich kid with no sense of reality. Not motivated to go to med school? Well, you don't have to! Generally unmotivated? Take a damn gap year! Spend some of that money to travel. Go to Moz for a month and get a dose of reality. Go teach English in a foreign country for a while. The thought that you are just going to permanently move to Moz right now is absurd. It is a fantastic country that I do I love, but you need to get out and experience reality before that sort of commitment

message:
Alright so I have finally got on a computer and I think yours is the one I should address first, although I think /u/wolfram184 has better points. 

First Malaria. You make a point of this and recently they have come out with a [vaccine](http://www.sciencetimes.com/articles/7520/20151019/malaria-vaccine-cure-cancer.htm) although it is highly debated and WHO has been wary recommending it, but at any rate I'm willing to bet that even if this vaccine is dangerous/ not effective it is a very good first step, and will likely lead to an effective on in a few years.

The corruption is a good point, but I'd like to ask where exactly you lived in Mozambque. I am skeptical that it would happen in the richer beach communities but I may be wrong, it may actually be more prevalent in them as they have money. 

I'm not going to lie, I am a spoiled rich kid who has never had to deal with the real life and neither has my family for a long time, but old money goes fast. After myself there probably wont be much of any money left if I stay in America, unless I make it rich. So I want to try and buy the best lifestyle that I can with what I have. To me that is living in a nice house on the beach, not in Suburbia. 

I agree with your assessment that I should first visit, but again I think /u/wolfram184 contributed and made better points.

@swift-complement, I've been to Moz too, but you're painting a pretty bleak picture. I had my fair share of adventures, but I never felt like my life was in constant danger. And yeah, police can be a pain, but I've had worse experiences in the US. As for the drug dealer incident, that's a one-off, right? I've met plenty of friendly locals who'd help me with car trouble.

@electric-puffin, Oh wow, a vaccine for malaria? That's news to me! I must've missed that in my 4 months there. Guess I'll just have to take my chances with the old "don't get bitten" strategy. And as for the corruption, I was in Maputo, so I'd say it's pretty prevalent there too. But hey, who am I to judge? I'm just a "sheltered, spoiled, rich kid" right? 🙄

message:
Only if by bullshit, you mean the [consenus opinion of economists](http://www.budget.gov.ie/Budgets/2015/Documents/Literature_Review_Economic_Effects_Corporation.pdf), sure.

>They will always spend as little as possible on expenses to maximize profits.

you need to think through this.  Company X gets a bunch more money because taxes go down.   what can they do with it?  Well, they can expand their business, which means hiring new people, which means paying more wages.  They can buy more products, which means other companies have to expand their businesses and hire more people, or they can invest it, which means buying  trading cash for capital, which means that some other company now has a bunch of extra cash and faces the same three options.  Companies do not keep money in scrooge mcduck styles vaults. They invest it, in something, and eventually, that means more hiring, which means wages go up.

"@soft-pilaster, you're missing the point. It's not about what companies 'should' do, it's about what they actually do. They hoard cash, buy back stocks, and pay dividends. Wages? That's just a cost they'd rather minimize. Economics 101, buddy."

message:
>1st. The alleged assault took place 36 years ago. The first time she even mentioned it to anybody was in 2012, 30+ years after it supposedly happened.

Would you prefer to start with the priest abuse scandal (which included many young people who were victims of sexual assault, and who didn’t come forward for many decades?

Or with Bill Cosby?

The idea that “well it happened a long time ago therefore it didn’t happen” has been repeatedly shown to be demonstrably false in cases involving sex crimes.

>The “victim” hasn’t been able to tell us where or what day it happened. Only that it was at a party that summer and she has changed her story multiple times.

Do you have any examples of substantial changes to her story?

More importantly, if it were completely made up wouldn’t it be pretty easy for her to say “it happened at this time, at this party, at this place” and make up a time and place where she knew Kavanaugh was, or couldn’t deny being?

Honest question:

If she had those details, if she had absolutely spot-on recall, would you not be a bit suspicious of how much she remembered?

>The only supposed witness (Mark Judge) has denied that any of this happened.

Who allegedly is also an accomplice to the crime. Do you really need to take a huge leap of logic to think that someone accused of helping his buddy commit rape would deny having committed a crime?

Judge isn’t exactly a neutral witness.

>Democrats held onto information about this accusation for 8 weeks

Well, no. They forwarded it to the FBI and local authorities, as well as encouraging the woman to come forward to testify.

And, again, if they’d released it immediately as a letter from a woman who didn’t want to come forward, would you not use *that* as evidence of a fabrication?

As in “whoa whoa whoa, they didn’t spend any time investigating it, and the woman isn’t coming forward, did the Democrats just make it up themselves?”

>Sixty five woman who knew Brett Kavanaugh in High School came out telling the press that he “has behaved honorably and treated women with respect.”

Cool!

Which is evidence that he couldn’t have done something horrible to a 66th? Lots of women who weren’t raped by Bill Cosby couldn’t believe the idea that he’d do anything to a woman. And millions of people who weren’t abused by priests would have sworn up and down that no priest would do that.

>The FBI refuses to investigate it because there is so little evidence that they don’t know where they’d begin

That’s not at all accurate. The FBI has no jurisdiction to investigate or enforce claims which would be brought under state law. They forwarded the information to the appropriate jurisdiction.

>People aren’t going to believe woman when something actually happens if they keep pushing ridiculous false accusations like these.

It sounds an awful lot like there’s not a set of circumstances in which you would have believed “woman who suffered abuse didn’t want to come forward and face the recrimination, harassment, shame, and doubt until it turned out her abuser was going to become one of the most powerful people in the country.”

@rubbery-terminal, you're missing the point entirely. It's not about whether she can recall every detail, it's about the principle of innocent until proven guilty. You're trying to play judge and jury here, and that's not how our justice system works.

message:
> including to show the world we're willing to use our military, to keep our military combat-ready and sharp

You can’t seriously be convincing me that we should fight in wars we don’t belong in so North Korea is scared of us.  What about the $700B war budget?  That’s pretty scary. 

> First being, "joining to kill".

I was quoting a comedian deep into a thread where I was specifically asked to opine a belief that I wasn’t even arguing.

> but that doesn't mean you shouldn't give a shit about them after a wreck happens

I’m mostly anti-Death, in general, I’m not going to reward someone for doing something foolish.

> Again OP, your comments make you sound not like you want your mind changed but that you want to change everybody else's mind.

Oh, please, I was expecting this to get downvoted into the ground, I had no desire or belief that I would change anyone’s view.  I got hit with about 10+ unique arguments, none of which really swayed my underlying issue with “supporting the troops”. I understand that we can’t live in a world with no conflict.  I simply think we are too zealous when it comes to conflict and everyone involved shares responsibility.  I could see changing my mind, but it becomes a little difficult because anyone who disagrees with me gets very emotional about it, very quickly.    

> And somebody has to do it to keep YOU from being conscripted, OP

Please don’t threaten me with conscription.  That’s getting close to asking Gay people to thank Straight people for doing all the procreation.

I give up on this. OP sounds like they're trying to ram their political feelings about the nature of military intervention into this beyond the point that sort of convincing is capable. They seem convinced that at least most service members join to "kill people that like killing people", that the military proper is some sort of meat grinder for sheep that are just stupid adults signing away their rights knowing they can get blown up because of a war-driven political party, and that the service members themselves are responsible for wars (none of which are "just" for a few decades). OP has expressed that their opinion stems from a preference to other styles of diplomacy with absolutely no indication that other styles of diplomacy are viable at all times, as well as the vast amount of money the military gets. Likely more, but I'm going to refute each one of these points to the best of my ability after laying some groundwork.

So to start, there is as some would say, "fetishizing". As somebody in an Army Public Affairs position, it's true. A bit of pride is fine, bragging and trying to get favors is obnoxious. "Quiet Professionalism" is the ideal military members are supposed to follow.

Next ground piece I'll talk about, as some have covered, is the Draft. Volunteering service members keep the Draft away as well as throw themselves to thew whim of politicians and circumstance. Are all wars fought for "your freedoms"? That's arguable, but you can be damn sure that when politicians want a war and have the support drummed up in the population without numbers to fight, you better get ready for Uncle Sam to send a job offer in the mail that you can't refuse.

Final piece I'll talk about is international diplomacy. Having a military, used or not, is a strong diplomatic piece. Our allies like our troops training with them and being stationed with them. South Korea has surrendered command of it's military in case the North strikes at them. We're the "deterrent", and despite some of our interventions being shady as fuck, there are both good and bad reasons for them, including to show the world we're willing to use our military, to keep our military combat-ready and sharp, to assist in what can at least be found to be intervention situations, to further political agendas for American interests, and to hopefully keep a "bigger" war from happening.

Now, back to my original points. First being, "joining to kill". I've never met a single person who joined to kill, even if they joined to fight a war in the 9/11 fervor, which mind you, we weren't even sure WHO the hell attacked us at first (some thought it would lead to a war with a European power, for example, in the first few hours/days). The military does psychological evaluation for people entering, and a strong desire to murder people is going to send you back to civilian life. I've met people who joined for college benefits, joined out of family tradition, joined to lift themselves out of economic hardship, joined to do more for their life, and a host of other reasons. We all have reasons, and they are everybody's own to know.

Next, the infamous "meat grinder" position on the military.  Many of the nation's brightest minds have seen voluntary service, including presidents, scientists, lawyers, engineers, doctors, artists, and everyday heroes. Again, everybody has their own reasons. War isn't decided by those reasons, it's decided by voters who elect politicians that are hungrier for war than the guy they run against in many cases. That's like saying a police officer joins the force because they want to see crime. Hell, maybe some do, but most people become cops, like service members, because they want to be part of a force that has a mission of good intention in the big picture. And a willingness to serve only in what sees as a "just" war is a form of conscientious objection, and if you are one, you better only join up when you see one of these wars you want to fight anyways. We can't simultaneously say these guys are brainwashed sheep as well as competent adults making their decision.

Next point of yours that I read is that you "prefer other forms of diplomacy". Nobody prefers that more than the average service member. But again, it doesn't come down to them, it comes down to voters and politicians. People in the military might volunteer to be subjected to war, but that doesn't mean you should say, "you signed up knowing this could happen" and not give a shit. Again, they knew it could happen, but that doesn't mean it's not still laudable to experience the horrors of war. People drive cars and get in wrecks sometimes, and they knew they could when they got in the car, but that doesn't mean you shouldn't give a shit about them after a wreck happens. Does all this mean the "lick my boots" fetishization of the military is okay? Hell no. But lack of respect shouldn't exist just because there's too much fervor from other people and it's seen as being too heavy or "for the wrong reasons". It's reactionary and silly to not like something just because you see a lot of people that are rabid fans.

Touching back on the point of other forms of diplomacy, certainly we've gotten ourselves into conflicts that we can argue were purely political and had nothing to do with defense. Was it part of a long-term agenda of keeping us sharp and being a deterrent, was it about war contracts, was it about doing good, was it about scoring points with the voters? Could be any combination, one of them to all of them, with a given politician, and again, it's up to the voters to decide on politicians with reasonable combinations of those reasons.

Again OP, your comments make you sound not like you want your mind changed but that you want to change everybody else's mind. Certainly you had your beliefs for a reason, but just because you have beliefs that have reason does not necessarily make them better. Not that mine do either, but when we only have subjective arguments to make against each other for the majority of this conversation, you need to be flexible and actually want to have your mind changed, and despite the understanding you'll likely offer a counterpoint or even a few to people trying to change your view, you have offered them endlessly and for varying reasons that you should've laid out in the post. I am convinced that short of a divine power or a somehow indisputable series of OBJECTIVE facts, you can't have your mind changed about the military deserving a high level of respect, even if a reasonable person can concede the jingoism of the last 30 years is far in excess of necessary or possibly appropriate given it's an institution of war instead of entertainment like a football team. 

When it comes down to it, people respect sacrifice, and regardless of the fact the average modern military member is voluntary and has not seen combat, they have put themselves in a position to serve their nation in roles that the government sees fit out of some need, some desire. And somebody has to do it to keep YOU from being conscripted, OP. Remember the next time you see a military ad that maybe you don't agree with any war we've had in 50 years and feel that if they get blown up in combat they knew it would happen, but you can at least go to bed knowing that because they volunteered to go, you weren't forced to.

message:
It's not a matter of returning to the glory days. It's a matter of giving people the opportunity to transition out or let people reach retirement. Forgive me if i am being presumptuous but you are probably in your 20s and your are also equipped with the foresight to know not to get into coal. Ball is in your court.

Imagine instead if you are 55 years old with 3 kids nearing or in college and when you got into coal it was the only reliable power source and was a secure future. What are you supposed to do now? You are 10 years from retirement in a field that evaporated very quickly. All you know is coal. You might have some skills that cross over into other fields but you are going to be looking at entry level work at the tail end of your career. 

Or you can vote for the guy that is gonna keep you employed into retirement. What are you gonna do?


I really sympathize with these people. My stepdad is a victim of the housing crash. He's been in construction for his entire working life and having to be unemployed  for multiple years of looking everyday changed him. You can't just tell someone in their 50s they need to start over or go back to school. Most of these people didn't have secondary education, favoring the trades. Its like they say if you don't use it you lose it. My ultimate point being that you are looking at this from the perspective of a young person. You could very well be in their shoes someday. That's worth thinking about.

@horizontal-pot, @steamed-chrome, sounds like we've got a couple of know-it-alls here thinking they can speak for everyone in the coal industry. Ever heard of a thing called "diversity"? Not everyone's cut out for college, and that's okay! Some of us are just fine with our calloused hands and hard hats. And hey, if you're so worried about retirement, maybe you should've thought about that before you picked up your first pickaxe. 😉

message:
>  If I want to make my point in the most crass, shocking way, that is my god damned right

And it's everyone else's "god damned right" to tell you off and ignore your point due to the way you presented it. Or to simply tell you that you're not welcome in their space due to your choice of language. 

> Moderators should use a light touch and let the public decide through upvotes and downvotes whether a post adds to the discussion.

Doesn't the level of activity on a given sub serve that purpose? If people object to that level of moderation, then they will stop using the sub. If people keep using the sub then the public has decided that that kind of moderation improves discussion. 

Essentially, your right to say what you want doesn't trump their right to moderate the tone of their space.

> Because censorship among adults is pretty fucking stupid

Refusing to interact with someone because they choose to utilize particular offensive language isn't censorship. It's just choosing who you want to associate with.

This is an interesting essay, but I'm not sure the distinction the author draws between the ultimate decision of the "imperial power of the state", the assessment of future historians and how a story is told is that compelling.

Generally there are different ways to portray many conflicts, depending on your point of view. [A recent New York Times piece](http://www.nytimes.com/2016/02/25/upshot/measuring-donald-trumps-supporters-for-intolerance.html?smid=tw-share&amp;_r=1) claimed that 38 percent of Donald Trump supporters wish that the South had won the US Civil War. 

It would be fair to assume that they do not see the Confederacy as the bad guys. I'm sure they could even point to the overwheening power of the union crushing a way of life and other reasons as justification for this distinction. In other words, there are many ways to tell a given story, depending on emphasis. And that is how history is written.

I think it's possible that you are taking the saying too literally as a tactical observation, while in reality it serves as an exclamation of moral objectivism, of a belief that *there is such a thing as justice*, and therefore we are moving towards it.

Sure, if you look at a fight for social justice on a level of personal events, you might see injustices happen. You might even see failed policies, lost battles, rolled back laws. 

But the point of the saying is not that liberals will always keep winning everything all the time, it's that there is such a thing as right and wrong, the world is not randomly swinging back and forth.

That even if injustice exists, it is defined as the temporary lack of of justice, not as the natural state of the world. 

There was a blog post about that matter, although it was addressed at the phrase "they are on the wrong side of history", it applies to this one as well. i'm going to quote a bit, but you should [read the whole thing](http://www.patheos.com/blogs/slacktivist/2015/07/02/try-not-to-be-the-bad-guy-in-the-story-what-it-means-to-be-on-the-wrong-side-of-history/).  


> I think the confusion here comes from the use of the word “history,” which these days connotes an academic discipline more than Clio’s art of story-telling. “The wrong side of history” isn’t really about that academic discipline — about the assessment of future historians. It’s about stories.

> “The wrong side of history” is not a claim about the future, but a claim about the present — about what’s happening right now. And it’s not a claim about winners or losers or about the inevitable progress of the dialectic. It’s about good guys and bad guys.

> Every rhetorical invocation of “The wrong side of history” is an invitation to ask Mitchell and Webb’s question: “[Are we the baddies?](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ToKcmnrE5oY)” (Or, if you want a more highbrow formulation, we can paraphrase Dickens: “Shall we turn out to be the heroes of our own story?”)

> The sheriff of Hadleyville was the good guy. The sheriff of Nottingham was the bad guy. Neither of those (probably) is a certifiably “historic” figure, but it still makes sense to say that former was on the right side of history while the latter was on the wrong side of it. Yes, these are “only” stories — not real history. But stories matter.

> The stories of the sheriff of Hadleyville and the sheriff of Nottingham inform how we evaluate the real-life story of the sheriff of Birmingham. Bull Connor was on the wrong side of history.

> That is obvious now, and it was obvious then. He was the baddie. We don’t say this because the imperial power of the state ultimately sided against him. We don’t say this because of some arrogant claim of inevitable, dialectical progress, or even because of some humbler assertion of eschatological faith in the long-bending arc of the moral universe. We say this because there’s no other way to tell this story.

@polynomial-dimension, I must admit, I'm struggling to follow your logic. Are you suggesting that Trump supporters are rooting for the bad guys in the story of the US Civil War? That's a bit of a stretch, isn't it? I mean, we're all just trying to make sense of history from our own perspectives.

> There is a cult of ignorance in the United States, and there always has been. The strain of anti-intellectualism has been a constant thread winding its way through our political and cultural life, nurtured by the false notion that democracy means that "my ignorance is just as good as your knowledge." - Isaac Asimov

The biggest problem in American politics is that a large segment of the population outright reject facts and evidence, and view intelligence and expertise as elitist. That's why you've got a whole party that rejects basic science and economics. In order to have a meaningful debate with someone, there need to be some common basis of reality from which both sides can draw and make reasoned arguments. Right now we don't have that. Instead we've got one side saying "the vast majority of evidence and all the smartest people on the planet are saying that climate change is a problem", and the other side saying "it's a Chinese hoax, and anyway, God wouldn't let anything bad happen." We can't hear you over the sound of us trying to write an entire tax bill in a matter of hours before it has to be voted on".

Until we can get everyone back on the side of evidence-based decision making (a term which the GOP has actually *banned* the CDC from using), no progress can be made, regardless of how each side views the other.

> What liberals are trying to get rid of due process?

I think it's important to note that /u/foot_kisser did not say liberals were trying to get rid of due process, he specifically stated **progressives**, which I agree with because due process is something that liberals have traditionally championed as an individual's right.

I think the best example would be the recent Kavanaugh confirmation hearings. Innocent until proven guilty and burden of proof are key components of due process and many people on the left disregarded them and were up in arms over Kavanaugh based only on uncorroborated accusations. 

Similarly, on college campuses there are many incidents of [Title XI where just being accused leads to repercussions against students, again without due process.](https://www.theatlantic.com/education/archive/2017/09/the-uncomfortable-truth-about-campus-rape-policy/538974/)

Conservatives are currently in the right in pushing for due process, and progressives are currently in the wrong for trying to get rid of it.  I purposefully didn't say convervatives are on the right side of history, because that phrase in and of itself endorses a specifically progressive narrative.  

"The right side of history" assumes that there is a "right side" of history, and that side is always towards the future.  A more accurate view of history is that "history is written by the victors".  Whoever wins the current culture war, for example, will declare that they were right all along.  So saying "I will end up being recorded in history books as being right" may or may not be true, but regardless of whether it's true, that doesn't make you right.  

Conservatives and progressives push and pull on society, tugging in opposite directions.  Neither side ever gets complete victory, nor should they.  Progressives are always pushing for changes they see as good, and they push hardest for the changes they think are the best.  Conservatives are always pulling back from changes they see as bad, and they pull hardest against changes they think are the worst.  As a result, the best changes generally happen, and the worst changes generally don't.  

If progressives always won, society would disintegrate, as they see little value in social cohesion or boundaries.  If conservatives always won, society would stagnate, as they see little value in changes.
